title: Alarming Data: How Scientists Should Speak When the News Is Catastrophic
summary: Two climate scientists at a Greenland research station disagree over whether alarming ice core findings should be communicated with full urgency or carefully calibrated to avoid the paralysis that catastrophising can trigger.

body.speaker: Dr. Ross
body.text: The core from sector fourteen is consistent with what we've been seeing all season, and at this point I don't think we can continue to prevaricate in the way the summary for policymakers requires us to. The rate of basal melt is two decades ahead of our worst-case projections.

body.speaker: Dr. Keller
body.text: I don't dispute the data. What I dispute is the inference from 'alarming' data to 'communicate this alarmingly'. Those are two separate decisions, and conflating them is not a scientific choice — it's a political one.

body.speaker: Dr. Ross
body.text: But so is the choice not to. The idea that calibrating your communication is a neutral act is itself a political choice, and it happens to be one that historically advantages those with a stake in delayed action. Every time we say 'further research is needed' or hedge our conclusions into apparent uncertainty, we provide cover for inaction.

body.speaker: Dr. Keller
body.text: I'm not arguing for hedging or obscuring the findings. I'm arguing for a distinction between what the data shows and what communication strategy best serves the goal of motivating action. And the research on this — the social psychology literature on risk communication — is fairly consistent: catastrophising triggers defensive mechanisms in audiences. When people hear that a situation is beyond recovery, they disengage rather than act.

body.speaker: Dr. Ross
body.text: I'm aware of that literature, and I think it's being misapplied here. The paralysis literature is primarily drawn from studies of individual-level risk perception — whether people act on their personal health risks. The mechanisms for collective action on existential risks may be fundamentally different. There is also a publication bias problem: we hear about the times that catastrophist framing caused paralysis; we don't hear about the times it galvanised action.

body.speaker: Dr. Keller
body.text: That's a fair point about the evidence base. But I think the deeper issue is about scientific authority. When scientists begin making explicit communication strategy choices — deciding not just what is true but how frightening to make it — we step outside our epistemic lane. We are trading scientific credibility for political effectiveness, and we may be making that trade without adequate training or legitimacy.

body.speaker: Dr. Ross
body.text: I find that argument troubling, frankly. It implies that scientists should report findings without any reflection on consequences — a kind of epistemic cowardice dressed as methodological rigour. Oppenheimer reflected on consequences. The epidemiologists who identified tobacco's link to cancer reflected on communication strategy when facing a well-funded campaign of denial.

body.speaker: Dr. Keller
body.text: Those are meaningful historical parallels, but they also illustrate the risk. The scientists who communicated the most effectively on tobacco — people like Doll and Hill — were extraordinarily careful about what they claimed and how they claimed it, precisely because they knew that the slightest overreach would be weaponised against them. The credibility of the science was the thing worth protecting.

body.speaker: Dr. Ross
body.text: And they were right to be careful in a context where the science was still developing. But the basal melt data is not contested within the field. We are not dealing with a situation of genuine scientific uncertainty; we are dealing with a communication environment in which manufactured uncertainty has been used to delay action. Those are different situations, and they may require different strategies.

body.speaker: Dr. Keller
body.text: I accept that distinction. But I still think the problem with urgency framing goes beyond paralysis. It also narrows the solution space. When climate is framed as a catastrophe that is already irreversible, it implicitly removes the incentive to prevent incremental additional harm. If we're already beyond the threshold, why does the choice between two degrees and three degrees matter? It matters enormously, of course — but the catastrophist frame makes it harder to communicate why.

body.speaker: Dr. Ross
body.text: That is the most substantive objection you've raised, and I don't have a clean answer to it. I think the honest communication of this data looks like this: the situation is worse than we projected, the window for the most effective action has narrowed significantly, but meaningful harm reduction remains possible within the window that exists. That is neither catastrophising nor minimising — it is what the data actually shows.

body.speaker: Dr. Keller
body.text: I can work with that framing. What I can't work with is language that implies foreclosure — that the situation is beyond recovery — because that is both scientifically imprecise and strategically counterproductive. The ice sheet doesn't care how we frame it. But the policymakers and publics who have to act on our findings do.

body.speaker: Dr. Ross
body.text: Agreed. Which is precisely why the framing matters — and why I think scientists who refuse to engage with that question on grounds of epistemic purity are making a political choice as surely as those who engage with it. The only position that isn't a political choice is the one that doesn't exist.

annotations.meaningVi: nói quanh co, tránh né sự thật một cách không trung thực
annotations.exampleEn: The minister continued to prevaricate when asked directly about the policy change.

annotations.meaningVi: tan chảy đáy băng — quá trình nước biển ấm làm tan chảy phần dưới cùng của tảng băng
annotations.exampleEn: Basal melt is less visible than surface melt but drives far greater ice sheet loss.

annotations.meaningVi: dự báo tồi tệ nhất — kịch bản tiêu cực nhất trong các mô hình dự đoán khoa học
annotations.exampleEn: The measured data consistently exceeded worst-case projections by a significant margin.

annotations.meaningVi: phạm vi nhận thức — lĩnh vực tri thức mà một người có thẩm quyền để phán xét
annotations.exampleEn: Staying in your epistemic lane means making claims only in areas where you have genuine expertise.

annotations.meaningVi: sự hèn nhát nhận thức — cố tình mơ hồ hoặc né tránh để không phải đưa ra phán xét rõ ràng
annotations.exampleEn: Vague academic conclusions that hedge every claim are often a form of epistemic cowardice.

annotations.meaningVi: thảm họa hóa — trình bày tình huống theo cách cực kỳ tiêu cực, thường đến mức gây ra lo lắng hoặc vô vọng
annotations.exampleEn: Catastrophising in risk communication can lead audiences to conclude that action is futile.

annotations.meaningVi: cơ chế phòng thủ — phản ứng tâm lý tự bảo vệ, trong ngữ cảnh này là sự từ chối hoặc tê liệt khi đối mặt với thông tin đáng sợ
annotations.exampleEn: Terror management theory explains how people employ defensive mechanisms when reminded of mortality.

annotations.meaningVi: sai lệch xuất bản — xu hướng công bố kết quả tích cực hoặc ấn tượng nhiều hơn kết quả âm tính
annotations.exampleEn: Publication bias in medical research means that failed drug trials rarely make it into the literature.

annotations.meaningVi: hành động được kích thích — sự thúc đẩy mạnh mẽ đến hành động, thường qua cú sốc hoặc khủng hoảng
annotations.exampleEn: The disaster galvanised action that years of campaign messaging had failed to produce.

annotations.meaningVi: uy tín khoa học — mức độ tin cậy và thẩm quyền của một nhà khoa học hoặc kết quả nghiên cứu
annotations.exampleEn: Scientists who make predictions that don't materialise risk losing their scientific credibility.

annotations.meaningVi: vượt quá giới hạn — đưa ra tuyên bố hoặc hành động vượt quá những gì bằng chứng cho phép
annotations.exampleEn: The report's conclusions were a significant overreach beyond what the data actually supported.

annotations.meaningVi: bị dùng như vũ khí chống lại họ — thông tin được đối thủ dùng để phá hoại uy tín
annotations.exampleEn: Any ambiguity in the findings was immediately weaponised against them by industry lobbyists.

annotations.meaningVi: sự không chắc chắn được tạo ra nhân tạo — chiến lược tạo ra nghi ngờ về khoa học đã được thiết lập để trì hoãn chính sách
annotations.exampleEn: The tobacco industry pioneered the strategy of manufactured uncertainty that the fossil fuel sector later adopted.

annotations.meaningVi: không gian giải pháp — phạm vi các hành động hoặc chính sách có thể được coi là khả thi
annotations.exampleEn: Framing the problem as binary narrows the solution space unnecessarily.

annotations.meaningVi: tác hại bổ sung dần dần — thiệt hại thêm theo từng bước nhỏ, nhưng quan trọng về tổng thể
annotations.exampleEn: Even if the worst outcomes are unavoidable, preventing incremental additional harm remains a moral obligation.

annotations.meaningVi: sự đóng cửa hoàn toàn — ý nghĩa rằng mọi lựa chọn đều đã bị loại bỏ, không còn hy vọng
annotations.exampleEn: Language of foreclosure — 'it's already too late' — undermines the case for any action.

annotations.meaningVi: thuần khiết nhận thức — việc tập trung hoàn toàn vào sự thật và từ chối xem xét tác động xã hội của thông tin
annotations.exampleEn: Epistemic purity — refusing to consider how findings are framed — ignores the scientist's role in society.

annotations.meaningVi: nhận thức về rủi ro — cách cá nhân đánh giá xác suất và mức độ nghiêm trọng của mối nguy hiểm
annotations.exampleEn: Risk perception studies show that dread risks — nuclear, pandemic — are systematically overestimated.

annotations.meaningVi: tóm tắt cho các nhà hoạch định chính sách — tài liệu được IPCC chuẩn bị để dịch các phát hiện khoa học cho khán giả chính sách
annotations.exampleEn: The IPCC's Summary for Policymakers is often more cautiously worded than the underlying scientific reports.

annotations.meaningVi: Richard Doll và Austin Bradford Hill — nhà nghiên cứu đã chứng minh liên hệ giữa thuốc lá và ung thư phổi vào những năm 1950
annotations.exampleEn: Doll and Hill's 1950 study was among the first to establish a statistical link between smoking and lung cancer.

grammarNotes.title: Gerund subjects and nominalisation in argument — 'Catastrophising triggers…', 'Framing it as…'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Gerund làm chủ ngữ (gerund subject) biến một hành động thành chủ đề có thể tranh luận. 'Catastrophising triggers defensive mechanisms' — biến hành động thảm họa hóa thành một thực thể có thể được đánh giá là đúng hay sai. Tương tự, 'Framing climate as a catastrophe… removes the incentive to prevent incremental additional harm' — gerund phrase 'Framing climate as a catastrophe' làm chủ ngữ cho phép Keller tranh luận về một chiến lược giao tiếp cụ thể. Đây là cấu trúc phổ biến trong tranh luận học thuật khi đánh giá hành động hoặc phương pháp.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Gerund subjects allow processes, strategies, and practices to become the subject of evaluation: 'Catastrophising triggers...', 'Framing it as...', 'Refusing to engage...'. Each gerund subject makes a communication strategy debatable rather than assumed — it becomes an agent that can be judged, defended, or criticised. Note also 'the only position that isn't a political choice is the one that doesn't exist' — a philosophical claim using a defining relative clause ('that doesn't exist') to eliminate a hypothetical exception.

grammarNotes.title: Reporting speech and attribution — 'associated with', 'in Cacioppo's formulations', 'the research… is fairly consistent'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong tranh luận khoa học và học thuật, người ta thường trích dẫn nghiên cứu hoặc tác giả không bằng cách trích trực tiếp mà bằng cách quy kết gián tiếp. 'One interpretation, associated with the psychologist Jean Twenge' — không phải trích dẫn trực tiếp mà là quy kết một quan điểm cho một tác giả cụ thể, cho phép diễn giả khoảng cách để đánh giá hay bác bỏ nó. 'The research on this… is fairly consistent' — dùng 'fairly consistent' thay vì 'proves' để thể hiện sự cẩn thận học thuật.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Academic and scientific English relies heavily on attributed reporting: 'associated with', 'in X's formulations', 'the literature is fairly consistent'. These phrases create epistemic distance — the speaker is reporting a position rather than asserting it as absolute truth. Note how Ross says 'I'm aware of that literature, and I think it's being misapplied here' — he accepts the literature's existence while contesting its application, a move that preserves intellectual credibility while maintaining disagreement.

translationVi:
Dr. Ross: Lõi từ khu vực mười bốn nhất quán với những gì chúng ta đã thấy suốt mùa này, và tại thời điểm này tôi không nghĩ chúng ta có thể tiếp tục nói quanh co theo cách mà tóm tắt cho các nhà hoạch định chính sách yêu cầu. Tốc độ tan chảy đáy băng đang đi trước hai thập kỷ so với dự báo tồi tệ nhất của chúng ta.
Dr. Keller: Tôi không tranh cãi về dữ liệu. Điều tôi tranh cãi là suy luận từ dữ liệu 'đáng báo động' sang 'truyền đạt điều này một cách đáng báo động'. Đó là hai quyết định riêng biệt, và việc nhầm lẫn chúng không phải là lựa chọn khoa học — đó là lựa chọn chính trị.
Dr. Ross: Nhưng không làm vậy cũng vậy. Ý tưởng rằng việc hiệu chỉnh giao tiếp của bạn là một hành động trung lập bản thân nó là lựa chọn chính trị, và tình cờ đây là lựa chọn lịch sử có lợi cho những người có cổ phần trong việc trì hoãn hành động. Mỗi khi chúng ta nói 'cần nghiên cứu thêm' hoặc bao bọc kết luận của mình trong sự không chắc chắn có vẻ như, chúng ta cung cấp nơi ẩn náu cho sự bất hành động.
Dr. Keller: Tôi không lập luận cho việc che giấu hay làm mờ các phát hiện. Tôi đang lập luận cho sự phân biệt giữa những gì dữ liệu cho thấy và chiến lược giao tiếp nào phục vụ tốt nhất mục tiêu thúc đẩy hành động. Và nghiên cứu về điều này — tài liệu tâm lý học xã hội về giao tiếp rủi ro — khá nhất quán: thảm họa hóa kích hoạt cơ chế phòng thủ ở khán giả. Khi mọi người nghe rằng một tình huống đã vượt quá khả năng phục hồi, họ rút lui thay vì hành động.
Dr. Ross: Tôi biết tài liệu đó, và tôi nghĩ nó đang bị áp dụng sai ở đây. Tài liệu về sự tê liệt chủ yếu được rút ra từ các nghiên cứu về nhận thức rủi ro ở cấp độ cá nhân — liệu mọi người có hành động với rủi ro sức khỏe cá nhân của họ không. Các cơ chế cho hành động tập thể về rủi ro hiện sinh có thể hoàn toàn khác nhau. Cũng có vấn đề về sai lệch xuất bản: chúng ta nghe về những lần cách đóng khung thảm họa gây ra sự tê liệt; chúng ta không nghe về những lần nó kích thích hành động.
Dr. Keller: Đó là điểm công bằng về cơ sở bằng chứng. Nhưng tôi nghĩ vấn đề sâu hơn là về thẩm quyền khoa học. Khi các nhà khoa học bắt đầu đưa ra các lựa chọn chiến lược giao tiếp rõ ràng — quyết định không chỉ điều gì là đúng mà cái gì là đáng sợ thế nào — chúng ta bước ra ngoài phạm vi nhận thức của mình. Chúng ta đang đánh đổi uy tín khoa học để lấy hiệu quả chính trị, và chúng ta có thể đang thực hiện trao đổi đó mà không có đào tạo hoặc tính hợp pháp đầy đủ.
Dr. Ross: Tôi thấy lập luận đó đáng lo ngại, thành thật mà nói. Nó ngụ ý rằng các nhà khoa học nên báo cáo các phát hiện mà không có bất kỳ suy ngẫm nào về hậu quả — một loại hèn nhát nhận thức được che bằng sự nghiêm ngặt phương pháp. Oppenheimer đã suy ngẫm về hậu quả. Các nhà dịch tễ học đã xác định mối liên hệ của thuốc lá với ung thư đã suy ngẫm về chiến lược giao tiếp khi đối mặt với chiến dịch phủ nhận được tài trợ tốt.
Dr. Keller: Đó là những tương đồng lịch sử có ý nghĩa, nhưng chúng cũng minh họa rủi ro. Những nhà khoa học đã giao tiếp hiệu quả nhất về thuốc lá — những người như Doll và Hill — cực kỳ cẩn thận về những gì họ tuyên bố và cách họ tuyên bố, chính xác vì họ biết rằng sự lạm dụng nhỏ nhất sẽ bị dùng như vũ khí chống lại họ. Uy tín của khoa học là thứ xứng đáng được bảo vệ.
Dr. Ross: Và họ đã đúng khi cẩn thận trong bối cảnh khoa học vẫn đang phát triển. Nhưng dữ liệu tan chảy đáy băng không bị tranh cãi trong lĩnh vực này. Chúng ta không đang đối phó với tình huống bất ổn khoa học thực sự; chúng ta đang đối phó với môi trường giao tiếp trong đó sự không chắc chắn được tạo ra nhân tạo đã được sử dụng để trì hoãn hành động. Đó là những tình huống khác nhau, và chúng có thể đòi hỏi các chiến lược khác nhau.
Dr. Keller: Tôi chấp nhận sự phân biệt đó. Nhưng tôi vẫn nghĩ vấn đề với cách đóng khung cấp bách vượt ra ngoài sự tê liệt. Nó cũng thu hẹp không gian giải pháp. Khi khí hậu được đóng khung như một thảm họa đã không thể phục hồi, nó ngầm loại bỏ động lực để ngăn chặn tác hại bổ sung dần dần. Nếu chúng ta đã vượt qua ngưỡng, tại sao sự lựa chọn giữa hai độ và ba độ lại quan trọng? Nó quan trọng vô cùng, dĩ nhiên — nhưng cách đóng khung thảm họa làm cho việc giao tiếp tại sao lại khó khăn hơn.
Dr. Ross: Đó là phản đối thực chất nhất bạn đã nêu, và tôi không có câu trả lời rõ ràng cho nó. Tôi nghĩ giao tiếp trung thực về dữ liệu này trông như thế này: tình huống tồi tệ hơn những gì chúng ta dự báo, cửa sổ cho hành động hiệu quả nhất đã thu hẹp đáng kể, nhưng giảm thiểu tác hại có ý nghĩa vẫn có thể trong cửa sổ tồn tại. Đó không phải là thảm họa hóa cũng không phải là giảm thiểu — đó là những gì dữ liệu thực sự cho thấy.
Dr. Keller: Tôi có thể làm việc với cách đóng khung đó. Điều tôi không thể làm việc là ngôn ngữ ngụ ý sự đóng cửa hoàn toàn — rằng tình huống đã vượt quá khả năng phục hồi — vì điều đó vừa không chính xác về mặt khoa học vừa phản tác dụng về mặt chiến lược. Tảng băng không quan tâm chúng ta đóng khung nó như thế nào. Nhưng các nhà hoạch định chính sách và công chúng phải hành động dựa trên các phát hiện của chúng ta thì có.
Dr. Ross: Đồng ý. Đó chính xác là lý do tại sao cách đóng khung quan trọng — và tại sao tôi nghĩ các nhà khoa học từ chối tham gia vào câu hỏi đó với lý do thuần khiết nhận thức đang đưa ra lựa chọn chính trị chắc chắn như những người tham gia vào nó. Vị trí duy nhất không phải là lựa chọn chính trị là vị trí không tồn tại.

questions.prompt: What does Dr. Ross mean when she says the basal melt rate is 'two decades ahead of our worst-case projections'?
options: The melt rate is twenty years slower than expected.
The observed melt is as bad as the models predicted for twenty years in the future — the situation is far worse than anticipated.
The data was collected using equipment that was two decades old.
Scientists need another two decades of data before drawing conclusions.
questions.explanation: Being 'ahead of projections' means the actual situation has surpassed what was modelled — in this case, twenty years earlier than the worst-case scenario predicted it would reach this point.
questions.hint: 'Ahead of' in the context of projections means the reality is worse than predicted.

questions.prompt: Dr. Keller argues that choosing how alarmingly to communicate findings is a political, not a scientific, decision. Why does Ross challenge this?
options: Because scientists have a democratic obligation to inform the public.
Because the choice NOT to communicate alarmingly is equally political — and historically advantageous to those who benefit from delayed action.
Because scientific credibility requires engaging with political communication strategies.
Because the IPCC guidelines specifically recommend alarm-based communication.
questions.explanation: Ross argues: 'so is the choice not to. The idea that calibrating your communication is a neutral act is itself a political choice, and it happens to be one that historically advantages those with a stake in delayed action.'
questions.hint: Ross's key move is to apply Keller's own logic to the opposite choice.

questions.prompt: What does the social psychology literature on risk communication consistently suggest, according to Keller?
options: That urgency framing motivates greater public action.
That catastrophising triggers defensive mechanisms, leading to disengagement rather than action.
That audiences respond best to technical, data-heavy presentations.
That personal testimonials are more effective than statistical information.
questions.explanation: Keller states: 'the research on this — the social psychology literature on risk communication — is fairly consistent: catastrophising triggers defensive mechanisms in audiences. When people hear that a situation is beyond recovery, they disengage rather than act.'
questions.hint: Keller cites this literature to support his communication strategy argument.

questions.prompt: How does Ross challenge the application of the 'paralysis literature' to climate communication?
options: By arguing that social psychology research is fundamentally flawed.
By claiming that climate audiences are more educated and therefore more rational than average.
By noting the literature comes from individual health risk studies and may not apply to collective action on existential risks, and by raising the problem of publication bias.
By pointing out that Keller has not read the literature carefully.
questions.explanation: Ross says: 'The paralysis literature is primarily drawn from studies of individual-level risk perception… The mechanisms for collective action on existential risks may be fundamentally different. There is also a publication bias problem.'
questions.hint: Ross makes two separate challenges to the literature's applicability.

questions.prompt: What does Keller mean by scientists 'stepping outside their epistemic lane' when making communication strategy choices?
options: That scientists should only conduct research within their own discipline.
That scientists who decide how frightening to make findings are exercising a social and political judgment that goes beyond their scientific expertise and legitimacy.
That climate scientists are less qualified than social psychologists to discuss communication strategy.
That scientists should not communicate directly with the public.
questions.explanation: Keller argues: 'When scientists begin making explicit communication strategy choices — deciding not just what is true but how frightening to make it — we step outside our epistemic lane. We are trading scientific credibility for political effectiveness.'
questions.hint: 'Epistemic lane' means the domain of knowledge one has authority to speak within.

questions.prompt: Ross accuses Keller of 'epistemic cowardice dressed as methodological rigour'. What does this mean?
options: That Keller is hiding behind the complexity of the data to avoid admitting the situation is serious.
That refusing to reflect on the consequences of scientific communication, under the guise of staying 'pure', is actually a form of moral evasion.
That Keller has deliberately misrepresented the methodology to make his position sound more rigorous.
That methodological rigour is overvalued in climate science compared to communication skills.
questions.explanation: Ross argues: 'It implies that scientists should report findings without any reflection on consequences — a kind of epistemic cowardice dressed as methodological rigour.' The argument is that claiming to be 'above' communication strategy is not neutrality but evasion.
questions.hint: Ross is saying that what Keller presents as rigour is actually avoiding responsibility.

questions.prompt: What example does Keller use to argue that extreme caution in scientific claims protects rather than undermines scientific effectiveness?
options: The IPCC's cautious approach to climate projections.
The careful claims made by Doll and Hill in establishing the tobacco-cancer link.
Einstein's refusal to speculate beyond what his equations demonstrated.
The early epidemiology of COVID-19 transmission.
questions.explanation: Keller cites 'people like Doll and Hill — were extraordinarily careful about what they claimed and how they claimed it, precisely because they knew that the slightest overreach would be weaponised against them. The credibility of the science was the thing worth protecting.'
questions.hint: Keller names specific scientists from the tobacco research as models.

questions.prompt: How does Ross distinguish the tobacco science context from the current climate situation?
options: By arguing that the tobacco scientists were not sufficiently careful in their communication.
By noting that the basal melt data is not genuinely contested within the field — unlike tobacco science in its early stages, where genuine uncertainty existed.
By pointing out that the consequences of climate change are far more severe than those of tobacco.
By arguing that the manufactured uncertainty campaign around tobacco was more sophisticated.
questions.explanation: Ross says: 'they were right to be careful in a context where the science was still developing. But the basal melt data is not contested within the field. We are not dealing with a situation of genuine scientific uncertainty; we are dealing with manufactured uncertainty.'
questions.hint: Ross distinguishes genuine scientific uncertainty from manufactured uncertainty.

questions.prompt: What is Keller's 'most substantive objection', which even Ross concedes she doesn't have a clean answer to?
options: That catastrophising makes scientists appear untrustworthy to sceptical audiences.
That urgency framing narrows the solution space — by implying the situation is already irreversible, it removes the incentive to prevent incremental additional harm.
That climate models are not reliable enough to justify extreme communication.
That the IPCC process already ensures appropriate calibration of scientific claims.
questions.explanation: Keller argues: 'When climate is framed as a catastrophe that is already irreversible, it implicitly removes the incentive to prevent incremental additional harm. If we're already beyond the threshold, why does the choice between two degrees and three degrees matter?'
questions.hint: Ross explicitly says this is the 'most substantive objection' she has 'no clean answer to'.

questions.prompt: What communication approach does Ross propose as her final answer — neither catastrophising nor minimising?
options: Report only what is certain, and refuse to speculate about projections.
Acknowledge that the situation is worse than projected, the window has narrowed, but meaningful harm reduction remains possible.
Present two scenarios — optimistic and pessimistic — and let audiences choose.
Focus on solutions rather than problems to avoid triggering defensive mechanisms.
questions.explanation: Ross proposes: 'the situation is worse than we projected, the window for the most effective action has narrowed significantly, but meaningful harm reduction remains possible within the window that exists.'
questions.hint: Ross frames her approach as the accurate description of what the data shows.

questions.prompt: What does Keller mean when he says 'the ice sheet doesn't care how we frame it. But the policymakers and publics who have to act on our findings do'?
options: That policymakers are less rational than scientists and need to be managed.
That the physical reality is unchanged by communication choices, but communication choices determine whether that reality produces action — making framing a genuine ethical responsibility.
That scientists should prioritise policymaker preferences over scientific accuracy.
That ice sheet data should be communicated differently to different audiences.
questions.explanation: Keller is making a consequentialist point: physical facts exist independently of how they are communicated, but action depends on communication — therefore framing decisions have real-world consequences that scientists cannot escape.
questions.hint: Keller is acknowledging that framing matters — a concession to Ross's general position.

questions.prompt: Ross's final statement — 'The only position that isn't a political choice is the one that doesn't exist' — means what?
options: That scientists should openly declare their political positions before making scientific claims.
That there is no neutral, purely scientific position on communication strategy — all choices, including the choice not to consider strategy, are politically consequential.
That political affiliations inevitably contaminate scientific research.
That good science requires explicit engagement with political advocacy.
questions.explanation: Ross argues that Keller's appeal to 'epistemic purity' is itself a political position — the imagined neutral stance (purely scientific, no communication strategy) does not exist. All positions, including the refusal to engage, have political implications.
questions.hint: The logic is that if every position is political, claiming to be above politics is itself a political choice.

questions.prompt: How does Keller ultimately respond to Ross's proposed communication framing?
options: He rejects it as still too alarming.
He accepts it as workable, while maintaining his objection to language that implies foreclosure.
He agrees entirely and withdraws his earlier objections.
He insists that any deviation from the raw data summary is inappropriate.
questions.explanation: Keller says 'I can work with that framing. What I can't work with is language that implies foreclosure — that the situation is beyond recovery — because that is both scientifically imprecise and strategically counterproductive.'
questions.hint: Keller accepts part of Ross's position while maintaining his core concern.

questions.prompt: What is the significance of calling the denial campaign 'manufactured uncertainty'?
options: That the campaign made accurate claims about genuine scientific uncertainty.
That uncertainty in climate science is the same as uncertainty in tobacco science.
That the appearance of scientific uncertainty was deliberately created by industry interests to delay policy action, rather than arising from genuine scientific debate.
That scientific uncertainty should not be acknowledged in public communication.
questions.explanation: The term 'manufactured uncertainty' — associated with historian Naomi Oreskes's analysis of industry denial strategies — implies that the uncertainty presented to the public was strategically produced, not genuine. This is Ross's key distinction between the tobacco context and the current climate data.
questions.hint: Consider the difference between genuine scientific uncertainty and uncertainty created as a political strategy.

questions.prompt: What do both scientists ultimately agree on by the end of the conversation?
options: That urgency framing is always the most effective communication strategy.
That scientific findings should be communicated without any consideration of impact.
That framing matters — communication choices have consequences — and that language of foreclosure (implying no recovery is possible) is both scientifically imprecise and counterproductive.
That the IPCC's summary for policymakers is the appropriate vehicle for communicating the new findings.
questions.explanation: Keller explicitly says he can 'work with' Ross's framing and objects to foreclosure language as 'scientifically imprecise and strategically counterproductive'. Ross's final turn affirms that framing matters. Both converge on framing as consequential — the disagreement was about how, not whether, to engage.
questions.hint: Track both speakers' final positions on the framing question.

cloze.template:
Dr. Ross: The rate of basal melt is two decades ahead of our worst-case {{b1}}, and at this point we can't continue to {{b2}} in the way the summary for policymakers requires.
Dr. Keller: I don't dispute the data. I dispute the inference from 'alarming' data to 'communicate this alarmingly'. The idea that calibrating communication is a neutral act is itself a {{b3}} choice.
Dr. Ross: Every time we hedge our conclusions into apparent uncertainty, we provide cover for {{b4}}. The social psychology literature says {{b5}} triggers defensive mechanisms — when people hear a situation is beyond recovery, they disengage rather than act.
Dr. Keller: I'm aware of that literature. But it's primarily drawn from studies of individual-level {{b6}}. Mechanisms for collective action on existential risks may be fundamentally different. There is also a {{b7}} problem — we hear about paralysis; we don't hear about the times catastrophist framing galvanised action.
Dr. Keller: When scientists decide not just what is true but how frightening to make it, we step outside our {{b8}}. We trade scientific credibility for political effectiveness.
Dr. Ross: That is {{b9}} dressed as methodological rigour. Scientists like Oppenheimer and the tobacco epidemiologists reflected on consequences.
Dr. Keller: Doll and Hill were extraordinarily careful precisely because the slightest overreach would be {{b10}} against them. The credibility of the science was worth protecting.
Dr. Ross: But the basal melt data is not genuinely contested. We are dealing with a communication environment in which {{b11}} has been used to delay action — a different situation requiring different strategies.
Dr. Keller: Urgency framing also narrows the {{b12}}. If the situation is framed as already {{b13}}, it removes the incentive to prevent incremental additional harm — why does the choice between two degrees and three degrees matter?
Dr. Ross: The honest framing is: the situation is worse than projected, the window has narrowed significantly, but meaningful {{b14}} remains possible. That is neither catastrophising nor minimising.
Dr. Keller: I can work with that framing. What I can't work with is language that implies {{b15}} — that the situation is beyond recovery — because that is scientifically imprecise and strategically counterproductive.

options: predictions
models
projections
forecasts
blanks.explanation: 'Projections' — the specific term for future scenarios produced by climate models — is the word used in the text.

options: equivocate
obfuscate
prevaricate
hesitate
blanks.explanation: 'Prevaricate' — speak or act in an evasive way — is Ross's specific word for the hedging she objects to.

options: rhetorical
scientific
political
ethical
blanks.explanation: 'Political choice' is Keller's key distinction — calibrating communication is a political, not scientific, decision.

options: denial
delay
inaction
opposition
blanks.explanation: 'Inaction' is the specific consequence Ross identifies — hedged communication 'provides cover' for not acting.

options: urgency framing
alarm
catastrophising
fear messaging
blanks.explanation: 'Catastrophising' — the specific term from the social psychology literature — is what Keller says triggers defensive mechanisms.

options: collective risk
environmental risk
risk perception
risk communication
blanks.explanation: 'Individual-level risk perception' is the specific domain Ross says the paralysis literature draws from — not collective action on existential risks.

options: methodological
selection
confirmation
publication
blanks.explanation: 'Publication bias' — the tendency for only certain types of results to be published — is Ross's specific methodological critique of the paralysis literature.

options: scientific authority
professional role
epistemic lane
area of expertise
blanks.explanation: 'Epistemic lane' — the domain of knowledge one has legitimate authority within — is Keller's specific term.

options: scientific cowardice
intellectual dishonesty
epistemic cowardice
moral evasion
blanks.explanation: 'Epistemic cowardice' — deliberately vague or evasive claims to avoid controversy — is Ross's specific accusation.

options: cited
used
weaponised
deployed
blanks.explanation: 'Weaponised against them' — turned into an attack on their credibility — is the specific phrase used to describe how industry opponents exploit scientific overreach.

options: coordinated denial
disinformation
manufactured uncertainty
false balance
blanks.explanation: 'Manufactured uncertainty' is Ross's specific term — uncertainty deliberately created as a political strategy, not arising from genuine scientific debate.

options: policy options
range of responses
solution space
political choices
blanks.explanation: 'Solution space' — the range of feasible actions — is Keller's term for what urgency framing narrows.

options: catastrophic
beyond repair
irreversible
lost
blanks.explanation: 'Already irreversible' is the framing Keller objects to — it implies no future choice can prevent further harm.

options: prevention
mitigation
harm reduction
adaptation
blanks.explanation: 'Harm reduction' is Ross's specific term — not prevention (which implies stopping completely) but meaningful reduction of damage.

options: catastrophe
collapse
foreclosure
irreversibility
blanks.explanation: 'Foreclosure' — the closing off of all options — is Keller's specific term for the language he objects to in catastrophist communication.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Both Ross and Keller accept that all communication choices — including the choice to stay silent or hedge — are political acts with consequences. If this is correct, does it mean that scientists have a positive obligation to make communication strategy decisions, not just report findings? And if so, what expertise, training, or legitimacy would give a scientist the authority to make those decisions on behalf of the public?

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: The core from sector fourteen is consistent with what we've been seeing all season, and at this point I don't think we can continue to prevaricate in the way the summary for policymakers requires us to.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: The rate of basal melt is two decades ahead of our worst-case projections.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: I don't dispute the data.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: What I dispute is the inference from 'alarming' data to 'communicate this alarmingly'.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: Those are two separate decisions, and conflating them is not a scientific choice — it's a political one.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: But so is the choice not to.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: The idea that calibrating your communication is a neutral act is itself a political choice, and it happens to be one that historically advantages those with a stake in delayed action.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: Every time we say 'further research is needed' or hedge our conclusions into apparent uncertainty, we provide cover for inaction.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: I'm not arguing for hedging or obscuring the findings.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: I'm arguing for a distinction between what the data shows and what communication strategy best serves the goal of motivating action.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: And the research on this — the social psychology literature on risk communication — is fairly consistent: catastrophising triggers defensive mechanisms in audiences.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: When people hear that a situation is beyond recovery, they disengage rather than act.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: I'm aware of that literature, and I think it's being misapplied here.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: The paralysis literature is primarily drawn from studies of individual-level risk perception — whether people act on their personal health risks.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: The mechanisms for collective action on existential risks may be fundamentally different.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: There is also a publication bias problem: we hear about the times that catastrophist framing caused paralysis; we don't hear about the times it galvanised action.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: That's a fair point about the evidence base.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: But I think the deeper issue is about scientific authority.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: When scientists begin making explicit communication strategy choices — deciding not just what is true but how frightening to make it — we step outside our epistemic lane.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: We are trading scientific credibility for political effectiveness, and we may be making that trade without adequate training or legitimacy.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: I find that argument troubling, frankly.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: It implies that scientists should report findings without any reflection on consequences — a kind of epistemic cowardice dressed as methodological rigour.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: Oppenheimer reflected on consequences.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: The epidemiologists who identified tobacco's link to cancer reflected on communication strategy when facing a well-funded campaign of denial.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: Those are meaningful historical parallels, but they also illustrate the risk.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: The scientists who communicated the most effectively on tobacco — people like Doll and Hill — were extraordinarily careful about what they claimed and how they claimed it, precisely because they knew that the slightest overreach would be weaponised against them.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: The credibility of the science was the thing worth protecting.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: And they were right to be careful in a context where the science was still developing.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: But the basal melt data is not contested within the field.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: We are not dealing with a situation of genuine scientific uncertainty; we are dealing with a communication environment in which manufactured uncertainty has been used to delay action.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: Those are different situations, and they may require different strategies.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: I accept that distinction.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: But I still think the problem with urgency framing goes beyond paralysis.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: It also narrows the solution space.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: When climate is framed as a catastrophe that is already irreversible, it implicitly removes the incentive to prevent incremental additional harm.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: If we're already beyond the threshold, why does the choice between two degrees and three degrees matter?

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: It matters enormously, of course — but the catastrophist frame makes it harder to communicate why.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: That is the most substantive objection you've raised, and I don't have a clean answer to it.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: I think the honest communication of this data looks like this: the situation is worse than we projected, the window for the most effective action has narrowed significantly, but meaningful harm reduction remains possible within the window that exists.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: That is neither catastrophising nor minimising — it is what the data actually shows.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: I can work with that framing.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: What I can't work with is language that implies foreclosure — that the situation is beyond recovery — because that is both scientifically imprecise and strategically counterproductive.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: The ice sheet doesn't care how we frame it.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Keller
sentences.text: But the policymakers and publics who have to act on our findings do.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: Agreed.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: Which is precisely why the framing matters — and why I think scientists who refuse to engage with that question on grounds of epistemic purity are making a political choice as surely as those who engage with it.

sentences.speaker: Dr. Ross
sentences.text: The only position that isn't a political choice is the one that doesn't exist.
